idioms.definition: Equal or divided into two equal parts
examples: We split the restaurant bill fifty-fifty.
They shared the cost of the flat fifty-fifty.
The chances of winning are about fifty-fifty.
idioms.usageTip: Use 'fifty-fifty' when two people share something equally, or when the probability of two outcomes is roughly the same.

idioms.definition: An expression of happiness or relief at returning to your own home
examples: After two weeks abroad, I walked through the door and said 'home sweet home!'
Home sweet home – there's nowhere I'd rather be.
She dropped her bags and smiled: 'Home sweet home at last!'
idioms.usageTip: Usually said when arriving home after being away. It can be sincere or slightly ironic depending on tone.

idioms.definition: To feel relaxed and comfortable as if you were in your own home
examples: Please make yourself at home – the kettle's on.
He told his guests to make themselves at home while he finished cooking.
Make yourself at home; I'll be back in five minutes.
idioms.usageTip: This is a polite phrase hosts say to make guests feel welcome and comfortable.

examples: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today – I might skip the gym.
She looked under the weather, so we sent her home early.
He's been under the weather all week with a cold.
idioms.usageTip: Use this for mild illness. It's not appropriate for serious conditions. Very common in informal everyday speech.

idioms.definition: To relax and not work too hard; to calm down
examples: The doctor told me to take it easy for a few days after the operation.
Take it easy – there's no rush, we have plenty of time.
She decided to take it easy on her day off and just read a book.
idioms.usageTip: Can be used as advice to rest, as a farewell, or to tell someone to stay calm. Very versatile in informal contexts.

examples: I'm exhausted – I think I'll hit the hay early tonight.
It's almost midnight; we should hit the hay.
After the long drive, they hit the hay as soon as they arrived.
idioms.usageTip: This is an informal expression. Avoid it in formal writing. 'Hit the sack' means the same thing.

fillInExercises.sentence: They agreed to split the bill ___ so neither of them paid more than the other.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fifty-fifty' means divided equally between two people.

fillInExercises.sentence: After a long holiday, she finally walked through her front door and sighed, '___!'
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Home sweet home' expresses happiness or relief at returning to your own home.

fillInExercises.sentence: 'Sit down, have a cup of tea, and ___!' said the host with a warm smile.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Make yourself at home' is a polite invitation for a guest to feel comfortable.

fillInExercises.sentence: I've been ___ all week – I think I'm getting a cold.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Under the weather' means feeling slightly ill or unwell.

fillInExercises.sentence: You've been working non-stop for days. You really need to ___ this weekend.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take it easy' means to relax and avoid overworking yourself.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'm absolutely exhausted. I'm going to ___ as soon as I get home.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Hit the hay' is an informal expression meaning to go to bed.

conversations.title: Back Home After a Long Trip

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: [Home, sweet home|home sweet home]! Two weeks away feels like forever.

lines.speaker: Dad
lines.text: Welcome back! Come in and [make yourself at home|make yourself at home] – you know where everything is.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Thanks, Dad. To be honest, I've been a bit [under the weather|under the weather] since Tuesday. I think it was the food.

lines.speaker: Dad
lines.text: Oh no. Well, you should [take it easy|take it easy] tonight. No unpacking, just rest.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: That's the plan. By the way, Mia and I agreed to [split the travel costs fifty-fifty|fifty-fifty], so it wasn't too expensive.

lines.speaker: Dad
lines.text: Smart idea. Are you hungry? I can make something light.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Just some soup would be great. After that I'm going to [hit the hay|hit the hay]. I'm absolutely shattered.

lines.speaker: Dad
lines.text: Soup it is. It's good to have you back.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Hey everyone, come in! [Make yourselves at home|make yourself at home] – drinks are on the table.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Thanks for having us. I've been [under the weather|under the weather] all day, so a quiet evening is exactly what I need.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Perfect timing then. No wild plans tonight – let's all just [take it easy|take it easy].

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.text: Sounds good to me. Hey Carlos, do you want us to chip in [fifty-fifty|fifty-fifty] for the pizza later?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Don't worry about it tonight. [Home sweet home|home sweet home] means my rules – guests don't pay!

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Ha! You're too kind. I have to warn you though – I might [hit the hay|hit the hay] early. I'm exhausted.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: No problem at all. The sofa is yours if you want to doze off.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: divided equally between two people or sides
an expression of happiness at returning home
feel relaxed and comfortable as if in your own home
to relax and not work too hard

quizExercises.sentence: The chances of getting a place are roughly ___ – it could go either way.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Fifty-fifty' describes an equal split or equal probability between two outcomes.

quizExercises.sentence: I've been sneezing and coughing all day – I think I'm a bit ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Under the weather' means feeling slightly unwell or ill.

quizExercises.question: When would you most likely say 'make yourself at home'?
options: When you arrive at a hotel for the first time
When you welcome a guest into your house and want them to feel comfortable
When you return to your own home after a long trip
quizExercises.explanation: 'Make yourself at home' is said by a host to a guest to make them feel relaxed and welcome.

quizExercises.question: In which situation would someone say 'I'm going to hit the hay'?
options: They are about to start working on a big project
They are very tired and ready to go to sleep
They are going outside to do some gardening
quizExercises.explanation: 'Hit the hay' is an informal expression meaning to go to bed.
